title: Chance & the Long Run
subtitle: What a probability actually means
realWorldHook: An insurance company has no idea whether you'll crash your car this year — but across two million drivers, the crash rate barely moves.
conceptSummary: Probability is long-run relative frequency: unpredictable one at a time, predictable in bulk.

steps.title: Why insurers aren't gambling
steps.body:
An insurance company has no idea whether *you* will crash your car this year. You're one person — unpredictable.

But across two million drivers, the crash rate barely moves from year to year. That eerie stability is the only reason they can price a policy at all.

Probability is the math of exactly this: unpredictable one at a time, predictable in bulk.

steps.title: What a probability really is
steps.body:
When we say a die has a [[1-in-6 chance|A die has six equally likely faces, so over many rolls a six shows up about once every six times — roughly **16.7%**.]] of landing on a six, we don't mean the next roll is special.

We mean: roll it a *huge* number of times, and the fraction of sixes settles near 1/6.

A probability is a [[long-run relative frequency|The fraction of times an outcome happens if you repeat the experiment a *huge* number of times. That settled fraction is exactly what we call its probability.]]. Up close, randomness is lumpy. Far away, it's smooth.

steps.prompt: You roll a fair die 6 times. How many sixes do you expect to see?
steps.revealMessage: Hold that thought. Instead of arguing about it — let's actually run the dice and watch.

revealByOption.a: That's the gut answer — '1-in-6 over six rolls' *feels* like exactly one. But watch what six rolls actually do.
revealByOption.b: Good instinct — small batches scatter a lot. Let's see just how much.
revealByOption.c: Even narrower than reality — six rolls quite often turn up 2 or 3 sixes. Let's watch.

steps.prompt: Drag the slider to roll the die more and more times. Watch the bars as the number of trials climbs past a thousand.
At just a handful of rolls the bars were all over the place; by several thousand they sat close to 1/6 (about 16.7%). Why?

options.label: The die slowly becomes fair as you roll it
Small samples are noisy; the average settles as trials grow
Later rolls are more likely to be sixes to balance things out

feedback.correct: Right — randomness is lumpy up close and smooth far away. Six rolls is too few to trust; six thousand is plenty.
feedback.incorrect: Look at the spread of the early bars versus the late ones — the wobble shrinks as trials grow. That's the whole idea.

byOption.a: The die is fair the entire time — nothing about it changes. What changes is how much the *average* of many rolls wobbles.
byOption.c: That's the [[gambler's fallacy|The mistaken belief that past random results change the odds of future ones — like thinking a six is *due*. The die has no memory; each roll is still 1-in-6.]] — the die has no memory. Each roll is still 1-in-6; the average just steadies as you pile up more of them.

steps.hint: Compare how much the bars jump at 6 trials versus 6,000. What's actually shrinking?

steps.prompt: So back to the prediction: in just 6 rolls, how many sixes should you expect?

options.label: Often 0, 1, or 2 — small samples vary a lot

feedback.correct: Exactly. 'On average 1' does not mean 'exactly 1 each time.' Small samples swing wildly — that's why six rolls tell you almost nothing.
feedback.incorrect: Run the sampler again at a tiny number of trials. You'll see 0 sixes one time and 3 the next. The 1-in-6 only shows up in the long run.

byOption.a: If it were always exactly 1, there'd be no randomness at all. The expected value is 1, but any single batch of 6 scatters around it.

steps.hint: Expected value (1 six) is the long-run average, not a guarantee for one short batch.

steps.title: The long run, in one line
steps.body:
A probability is what a frequency settles to over many, many trials — an idea called the [[law of large numbers|The more times you repeat a random experiment, the closer the average result lands to the true probability. Small samples wander a lot; large ones barely budge.]].

That's why your single car is a mystery to an insurer but two million cars are a near-certainty. It's the same reason a casino can't predict one spin yet banks on the month.

Next up: what happens when you start combining chances together.
